skill.name: agent-tools
skill.description: Run 250+ AI apps via inference.sh CLI - image generation, video creation, LLMs, search, 3D, Twitter automation. Models: FLUX, Veo, Gemini, Grok, Claude, Seedance, OmniHuman, Tavily, Exa, OpenRouter, and many more.
skill.summary: Access 250+ AI models through a unified CLI interface for image, video, LLM, search, and automation tasks
skill.author: infsh-skills

security_audit.summary: This skill provides access to the inference.sh CLI for running 250+ AI apps. Static analysis detected many patterns (pipe to shell, external commands, hardcoded URLs) but evaluation confirms these are legitimate CLI tool patterns, not security vulnerabilities. The skill is properly scoped to belt commands only, uses official infrastructure, and implements standard installation verification (SHA-256 checksums, optional Sigstore signatures). No malicious behavior detected after semantic review.

high_findings.title: Pipe to Shell Installation Pattern
high_findings.description: The skill uses `curl | sh` pattern for CLI installation. This is a common installation method for the inference.sh CLI. The installer verifies SHA-256 checksums and optionally validates Sigstore signatures. While flagged as critical by static analysis, this is the intended and documented installation mechanism for the official CLI tool.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Pattern is real but represents standard CLI installation method with verification mechanisms in place

medium_findings.title: Network Access to External Service
medium_findings.description: The skill makes network requests to inference.sh infrastructure to run AI models. This is the intended functionality of the skill - accessing cloud-based AI services. No data exfiltration or unauthorized transmission detected.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Network access is required for the skill's core functionality and uses official infrastructure

medium_findings.title: API Key Environment Variable Access
medium_findings.description: The skill documents the INFSH_API_KEY environment variable for CI/CD authentication. This is a standard credential pattern for CLI tools. The variable is user-provided and not hardcoded.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Standard credential pattern for CLI authentication, requires user-provided keys

medium_findings.title: Local File System Access for Uploads
medium_findings.description: The skill supports local file paths for AI inputs (images, videos, audio). Files are uploaded to the inference.sh cloud for processing. No arbitrary file system operations beyond specified input paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: File paths are explicitly for AI input uploads, not arbitrary file access

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs to Official Infrastructure
low_findings.description: Multiple URLs to inference.sh domain are hardcoded in documentation. These are required for the CLI to function and point to official infrastructure. Not a security vulnerability.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: URLs are necessary infrastructure for CLI functionality, all point to official inference.sh domains

low_findings.title: Static Analysis False Positives on Code Patterns
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged 'weak cryptographic algorithm' for AI model names (FLUX, SDXL, Veo), 'shell backtick execution' for bash commands in documentation blocks, and 'path traversal' for relative path examples. These are all false positives from pattern matching without semantic context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Model names incorrectly flagged as crypto algorithms, documentation examples flagged as actual code execution

content.user_title: Run 250+ AI Models via inference.sh CLI
content.value_statement: Access hundreds of AI models for image generation, video creation, text processing, and automation without managing GPU infrastructure or API integrations.
actual_capabilities: Generate images using FLUX, Gemini 3 Pro, Grok, Seedream models
Create videos with Veo 3.1, Seedance, Wan 2.5, OmniHuman avatar
Call LLMs including Claude, Gemini, Kimi, GLM through OpenRouter
Perform web search via Tavily and Exa search APIs
Automate Twitter/X posting with post-tweet, post-create, dm-send
Generate 3D models and process local files for AI inputs
limitations: File uploads limited to supported image, video, audio formats
Task completion time varies by model complexity and queue
Some models may have rate limits or credit requirements

use_cases.title: Generate AI Images Without Local GPU
use_cases.description: Content creators and marketers can generate professional images using state-of-the-art models without installing and maintaining local AI tooling. Simply specify the model and prompt.

use_cases.title: Automate Social Media with AI-Generated Content
use_cases.description: Social media managers can programmatically post AI-generated images, videos, and text to Twitter/X. Create scheduled content workflows with consistent branding.

use_cases.title: Access Multiple AI Providers Through Single Interface
use_cases.description: Developers can experiment with different AI models by switching app names without changing code. Test FLUX, Claude, Gemini, and OpenRouter models with the same CLI syntax.
use_cases.target_user: Developers, AI researchers, technical product managers

prompt_templates.title: Generate an image with specific model
prompt_templates.prompt: Use belt to run falai/flux-dev-lora with the input {"prompt": "[DESCRIBE YOUR IMAGE]", "num_images": 1, "image_size": "landscape_16_9"}

prompt_templates.title: Create video from prompt
prompt_templates.prompt: Run google/veo-3-1-fast with {"prompt": "[VIDEO DESCRIPTION]"} and save the task ID for tracking
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate - video generation with task tracking

prompt_templates.title: Chain multiple AI tasks
prompt_templates.prompt: First generate an image with falai/flux-dev-lora, then use that output URL to create a video with bytedance/omnihuman-1-5 using the avatar prompt

prompt_templates.title: Search and analyze web content
prompt_templates.prompt: Use tavily/search-assistant to find recent information about [TOPIC], then use exa/extract to pull key details from the top results

output_examples.input: belt app run falai/flux-dev-lora --input '{"prompt": "a serene mountain lake at sunset"}'
output_examples.output:
Running falai/flux-dev-lora...
Task ID: abc123xyz
Status: completed

| App | Description |
|-----|-------------|
| falai/flux-dev-lora | FLUX.2 Dev (high quality) |

| xai/grok-imagine-image | Grok Image Generation |

output_examples.input: belt app run x/post-tweet --input '{"text": "Just generated this with #AI!"}'
output_examples.output:

Status: posted
Tweet ID: 1234567890
URL: https://twitter.com/user/status/1234567890

best_practices: Use `belt app sample` to generate input templates before running tasks
Save task IDs when using `--no-wait` to check status later with `belt task get`
Store your INFSH_API_KEY securely and prefer `belt login` for interactive authentication
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode API keys in scripts - use environment variables or `belt login`
Avoid running multiple large tasks simultaneously without tracking - use `belt task get` to monitor progress
Do not upload sensitive personal files - the CLI uploads files to cloud.inference.sh for processing

faq.question: How do I authenticate with inference.sh?
faq.answer: Run `belt login` to open a browser for authentication. For CI/CD, set the INFSH_API_KEY environment variable with your API key.

faq.question: What file formats are supported for uploads?
faq.answer: Common image formats (PNG, JPG, WEBP), video formats (MP4, MOV), and audio formats (MP3, WAV) are supported. Check specific app requirements with `belt app get <app>`.

faq.question: How do I track long-running tasks?
faq.answer: When you run an app, you'll receive a task ID. Use `belt task get <task-id>` to check status. Use `belt task get <task-id> --save result.json` to save results.

faq.question: Can I run multiple AI models in sequence?
faq.answer: Yes. Chain outputs by using URLs or file paths from one task as inputs to another. For example, use an image generation URL as input for a video generation app.

faq.question: How do I find available apps?
faq.answer: Use `belt app list` to see all apps. Filter by category with `--category image`, `--category video`, etc. Search with `belt app list --search "flux"`.

faq.question: Is the installation secure?
faq.answer: The installer verifies SHA-256 checksums and optionally validates Sigstore signatures. You can also manually download and verify binaries if you prefer not to pipe to shell.
